results.possibleIdentity: Murray S. Marcus (frequently cited as Murray Marcus) was an electronics engineer and technical program manager active during the 1960s and 1970s. He was employed by the Airborne Instruments Laboratory (AIL), a division of Cutler-Hammer located in Deer Park, Long Island, New York. Marcus was notably involved in the development of the Ionospheric Explorer XX (IE-XX) topside sounder spacecraft for NASA, which was launched in August 1964. His work on this project encompassed the design, fabrication, testing, and launch checkout of the fixed-frequency spacecraft. Professionally, he was an active member of the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE), where he contributed to the management and technical programming of large-scale electronic programs, including serving as a session chair or technical program committee member for IEEE international conventions in the mid-1960s. He is also identified as a technical person associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization founded by Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg to facilitate collaborations between artists and the scientific/engineering community, reflecting the typical profile of industry engineers who provided technical expertise for artistic projects during that era.
